back:
1) ACEi/ARB or ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan preferred)
2) Beta-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol ONLY)
3) MRA (spironolactone or eplerenone)
4) SGLT2i (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin)
All 4 reduce mortality in HFrEF.
36-hour washout from ACE inhibitor required before starting ARNI to prevent angioedema (both inhibit bradykinin degradation). No washout needed when switching FROM ARB to ARNI.

front: ACS: STEMI vs NSTEMI reperfusion strategy
back:
STEMI: emergent PCI (door-to-balloon <90 min) or thrombolytics if PCI unavailable (<120 min door-to-device)
NSTEMI/UA: risk-stratify; early invasive if high-risk (GRACE >140, dynamic ECG, elevated troponin)

Clopidogrel: prodrug (CYP2C19), variable response, cheapest
Ticagrelor: reversible, faster onset, preferred in ACS, causes dyspnea (10%)
Prasugrel: most potent; CONTRAINDICATED in prior stroke/TIA, age >75, wt <60kg
Ticagrelor preferred for ACS; clopidogrel used when others contraindicated

C=CHF(1), H=HTN(1), A2=Age>=75(2), D=DM(1), S2=Stroke/TIA(2), V=Vascular(1), A=Age 65-74(1), Sc=Female(1)
Anticoagulate: men >=2, women >=3
Consider: men=1, women=2
Do NOT use aspirin as substitute for anticoagulation in high-risk AFib

front: Apixaban dose reduction criteria in AFib
back:
Reduce to 2.5mg BID if patient has >= 2 of:
- Age >= 80 years
- Weight <= 60 kg
- SCr >= 1.5 mg/dL
Note: criteria-based (NOT CrCl-based like rivaroxaban/dabigatran)

INR 5-10, no bleeding: Hold 1-2 doses +/- vitamin K 1-2.5mg PO
Minor bleeding: Vitamin K 2.5-5mg PO
Major bleeding: 4F-PCC + IV Vitamin K 10mg
Dabigatran reversal: Idarucizumab (Praxbind) 5g IV
Factor Xa inhibitor reversal: Andexanet alfa (Andexxa)

HIGH (>=50% LDL reduction): Atorvastatin 40-80mg, Rosuvastatin 20-40mg
MODERATE (30-49%): Atorvastatin 10-20mg, Rosuvastatin 5-10mg, Simvastatin 20-40mg
LOW (<30%): Simvastatin 10mg, Pravastatin 10-20mg
High-intensity indicated: ASCVD, LDL>=190, DM age 40-75 with 10-yr risk >=7.5%

P = Pulmonary toxicity (ground-glass CTs, decreased DLCO) -- #1 killer
T = Thyroid dysfunction (both hypo AND hyperthyroidism)
C = Corneal microdeposits (reversible) + blue-gray skin + CYP interactions
H = Hepatotoxicity (LFTs)
Also: peripheral neuropathy, photosensitivity, QT prolongation
Monitor: PFTs, TFTs, LFTs, CXR annually

1st line: Norepinephrine (alpha1 > beta1)
2nd line: Add vasopressin 0.03-0.04 units/min (NE-sparing)
3rd line: Epinephrine (causes hyperglycemia + lactic acidosis)
Alternative 1st: Dopamine (more arrhythmias vs NE; avoid if tachycardic)
Inotrope for low CO: Dobutamine
Pure alpha: Phenylephrine (avoid in low cardiac output)

HFrEF (EF<40%): ACEi/ARB/ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i = mortality benefit
HFpEF (EF>=50%): SGLT2i primary Rx (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER); diuretics for congestion; no proven mortality benefit from ACEi/BB/MRA
Both benefit from: SGLT2i, diuretics for congestion
Avoid in HFrEF: verapamil/diltiazem (negative inotropes), NSAIDs, thiazolidinediones

front: ACC/AHA HTN first-line agents by indication
back:
CKD + proteinuria: ACEi or ARB
DM: ACEi or ARB (renal protection) + SGLT2i
HF: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + BB + diuretics
After MI: ACEi/ARB + BB
Black patients without compelling indications: thiazide or CCB (ACEi less effective as monotherapy)
Goal: <130/80 for most adults per 2017 ACC/AHA

Vasopressor: Norepinephrine (less arrhythmias vs dopamine - SOAP II)
Inotrope: Dobutamine (add to NE for forward flow)
Mechanical support: Impella or ECMO for refractory cases; IABP limited benefit in STEMI-CS
Treat cause: emergent PCI for STEMI
Avoid: phenylephrine (worsens CO), high-dose vasopressors without treating underlying cause
Corticosteroids: NOT routinely used

EF <=35% + NYHA Class II-III on optimal medical therapy x >= 3 months + life expectancy >1 year
Primary prevention: prevents sudden cardiac death
Secondary prevention: prior VT/VF cardiac arrest
CRT-D also: EF <=35%, LBBB with QRS >=150ms, NYHA II-III
Note: ICD NOT indicated for NYHA Class IV (unlikely to benefit from SCD prevention without transplant/LVAD)

front: DAPT duration after coronary stent placement
back:
ACS (STEMI/NSTEMI) + DES: minimum 12 months (aspirin + ticagrelor/clopidogrel/prasugrel)
Stable CAD + DES: 6 months minimum
Stable CAD + BMS: 1 month minimum
High bleeding risk: can shorten to 3-6 months with newer DES generation
After DAPT: aspirin indefinitely
Ticagrelor extension to 3 years (PEGASUS): prior MI + high ischemic risk

Provoked by transient reversible factor: 3 months total
Unprovoked first VTE: 3 months minimum; consider indefinite
Recurrent unprovoked VTE: indefinite
Cancer-associated VTE: LMWH or DOAC; treat duration of cancer activity
Antiphospholipid syndrome: indefinite warfarin INR 2-3 (DOACs inferior in triple-positive APS)

Timing: platelet fall days 5-10 after heparin exposure
Diagnosis: 4T score (Thrombocytopenia, Timing, Thrombosis, oTher causes)
STOP all heparin immediately (including line flushes)
NON-heparin anticoagulants: argatroban (hepatic clearance; preferred in renal failure), bivalirudin, fondaparinux
DO NOT use warfarin acutely (depletes protein C)
Transition to warfarin ONLY after platelets recover to >150k

LMWH: predictable PK; weight-based; no routine monitoring (check anti-Xa if obese/renal); preferred cancer-associated VTE; renally cleared (reduce dose CrCl <30)
UFH: aPTT monitoring required; titratable; preferred in renal failure; immediately reversible (protamine)
Protamine reversal: UFH fully; LMWH ~60-80%
Anti-Xa monitoring: both LMWH and UFH (preferred over aPTT in some centers)

Dabigatran: direct thrombin inhibitor; 80% renal; only DOAC with specific reversal agent (idarucizumab); requires capsule form integrity
Rivaroxaban: factor Xa; take 20mg WITH dinner (absorption)
Apixaban: factor Xa; least renal (~27%); preferred in CKD/ESRD; BID dosing
Edoxaban: factor Xa; avoid if CrCl >95 (paradoxically too low exposure in ENGAGE AF)
All 4: andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC (off-label) for reversal

Outpatient (no comorbidities): Amoxicillin 1g TID OR doxycycline 100mg BID
Outpatient (comorbidities): Amox-clav + macrolide OR resp FQ
Inpatient non-ICU: Beta-lactam + macrolide OR respiratory FQ
ICU: Beta-lactam + azithromycin OR beta-lactam + FQ; add anti-pseudomonal if risk factors; add vancomycin if MRSA risk

Sepsis: organ dysfunction (SOFA >=2) from infection
Septic shock: vasopressors needed + lactate >2 despite fluids
qSOFA (screening, not diagnosis): altered mentation + RR >=22 + SBP <=100 (2/3 = high risk)
1-hour bundle: lactate, cultures, abx, 30mL/kg fluids, vasopressors if needed

Non-severe: WBC <15k AND SCr <1.5 -- vancomycin 125mg QID x10d OR fidaxomicin 200mg BID x10d
Severe: WBC >=15k OR SCr >=1.5 -- same treatment
Fulminant: hypotension/shock/ileus -- vancomycin 500mg QID PO + IV metronidazole; surgery consult
Recurrent: fidaxomicin preferred; consider bezlotoxumab; FMT for multiple recurrences
Metronidazole NO LONGER recommended for any CDI (inferior outcomes)

Prior MRSA infection/colonization
Cavitary or necrotizing pneumonia
IV drug use
Preceding influenza
Severe CAP with septic shock
Recent hospitalization + prior antibiotics
Agents: vancomycin (AUC 400-600 mg*h/L) or linezolid (600mg q12h)

Azoles: inhibit ergosterol synthesis (CYP51/lanosterol demethylase)
- Fluconazole: Candida (not krusei), Crypto
- Voriconazole: Aspergillus, molds, Candida
- Posaconazole: broadest azole (Mucor coverage)
Echinocandins: inhibit beta-1,3-glucan synthase; first-line candidemia; NO Crypto coverage
Amphotericin B: binds ergosterol; broadest; nephrotoxic (use liposomal form)
Flucytosine: ONLY in combination with AmB (for Cryptococcus)

CD4 <200: TMP-SMX DS daily (PCP prophylaxis; also covers Toxo and some bacteria)
CD4 <100 (+ Toxo IgG+): TMP-SMX covers Toxoplasma (no additional drug needed if already on TMP-SMX)
CD4 <50: Azithromycin 1200mg weekly (MAC prophylaxis) or clarithromycin
Discontinue prophylaxis: PCP/Toxo when CD4 >200 x 3 months; MAC when CD4 >100 x 3 months on ART

Ceftriaxone: most GNR, NO Pseudomonas, no anaerobes
Cefepime: GNR + Pseudomonas, no anaerobes
Pip-tazo: GNR + Pseudomonas + anaerobes + GPCs (NOT MRSA)
Meropenem: broadest GNR + Pseudomonas + ESBL + anaerobes (carbapenem-sparing: use pip-tazo or ceftolozane/taz for Pseudomonas)
Aztreonam: GNR only including Pseudomonas; safe in ALL beta-lactam allergies EXCEPT ceftazidime

Preferred (higher completion rates):
1. 3HP: rifapentine + INH once weekly x 12 weeks (can be self-administered)
2. 4R: rifampin daily x 4 months
Alternative:
3. 9H: INH x 9 months
All require ruling out active TB first (symptom screen + CXR)
Screen before: anti-TNF therapy, transplant, high-dose steroids, HIV

Neonates: Ampicillin + cefotaxime (covers GBS, Listeria, Gram-negatives)
1-3 months: Ampicillin + cefotaxime
3 months to 50 years: Ceftriaxone + vancomycin
>50 or immunocompromised: Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin (Listeria)
Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h x 4 days (give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose; most benefit for S. pneumoniae)

Penicillin allergy + cephalosporins: <2% cross-reactivity (mainly R1 side-chain)
Penicillin anaphylaxis: skin test first; if negative, can try cephalosporin
Ceftazidime and aztreonam: shared R1 side chain (cross-reactive with each other)
Carbapenem + PCN allergy: <1% cross-reactivity
Practice: most PCN-allergic patients can safely receive cephalosporins with proper assessment

CrCl = [(140-age) x weight / (72 x SCr)] x 0.85 if female
Weight: IBW if TBW < IBW (use TBW)
IBW if TBW > IBW and not obese
AdjBW (= IBW + 0.4 x [TBW - IBW]) if obese (TBW > 30% above IBW)
IBW male = 50 + 2.3 x (ht inches - 60)
IBW female = 45.5 + 2.3 x (ht inches - 60)

Beta-lactams: %T>MIC (concentration-independent; 40-70% dosing interval)
Aminoglycosides: Cmax/MIC >=8-10 (concentration-dependent; once-daily extended-interval preferred)
Fluoroquinolones: AUC/MIC >125 (Gram-neg) or >30-50 (S. pneumoniae)
Vancomycin: AUC/MIC 400-600 mg*h/L (target MIC <=1 for MRSA)
Extended-infusion beta-lactams maximize T>MIC by prolonging infusion time

Target: AUC/MIC 400-600 mg*h/L (MRSA MIC <=1 mg/L)
Initial dose: 15-20 mg/kg actual body weight q8-12h
Do NOT use trough-only monitoring (unreliable)
Monitoring: 2-point sampling (peak 1-2h after infusion end + trough) for Bayesian AUC
Do NOT use if MRSA MIC >2 (inadequate AUC achievable)
Obesity: use IBW + 0.4(TBW-IBW) for initial dose estimation

Small Vd (<1 L/kg): drug stays in plasma; e.g., warfarin (0.1 L/kg), aminoglycosides (0.25 L/kg)
Large Vd (>5 L/kg): extensive tissue distribution; e.g., amiodarone (60 L/kg), chloroquine, digoxin (7 L/kg)
Large Vd = large loading dose needed = harder to remove by dialysis
t1/2 = 0.693 x Vd/CL; drugs with large Vd have long half-lives if CL is not correspondingly high

STRONG INDUCERS (decrease levels): Rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, St. John's Wort
STRONG INHIBITORS (increase levels): Azole antifungals, clarithromycin, ritonavir, amiodarone, grapefruit juice
Clopidogrel prodrug activation inhibited by: omeprazole, esomeprazole (CYP2C19 inhibitors) -- reduce antiplatelet effect
Statins + CYP3A4 inhibitors: myopathy risk (simvastatin most affected; pravastatin safest)

Corrected phenytoin = Measured / [0.2 x (albumin) + 0.1]
In renal failure (CrCl <10): = Measured / [0.1 x (albumin) + 0.1]
Therapeutic total phenytoin: 10-20 mcg/mL
Free phenytoin (unbound): 1-2 mcg/mL
Alternative: directly measure free phenytoin level (most accurate)
Phenytoin follows zero-order kinetics at toxic levels (small dose changes = large level changes)

First-order: rate proportional to concentration; constant FRACTION eliminated; linear PK; most drugs
Zero-order: constant AMOUNT eliminated (saturable enzymes); nonlinear PK; dose changes cause disproportionate level changes
Zero-order examples: phenytoin, ethanol, aspirin (high doses), warfarin at supratherapeutic levels
Clinical implications: small phenytoin dose increases can cause toxicity

F = fraction of dose reaching systemic circulation
IV = 1.0 (100%) by definition
High first-pass (low oral F): morphine (20-40%), nitroglycerin (~1% -- must use SL/patch/IV), lidocaine, propranolol
Sublingual bypasses first-pass: NTG, buprenorphine
Food effects: rivaroxaban absorption INCREASES with food (take with evening meal); certain drugs have reduced absorption with food

front: ADA diabetes drug selection by comorbidity
back:
ASCVD or high CV risk: GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide) OR SGLT2i with CV benefit
HF: SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin)
CKD with albuminuria: SGLT2i + finerenone
Weight loss priority: GLP-1 RA (semaglutide most) or SGLT2i
Hypoglycemia risk: avoid sulfonylureas; use DPP-4i, GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i
Cost-sensitive: metformin + sulfonylurea

Genital mycotic infections (most common; yeast due to glucosuria)
UTI (increased risk)
Volume depletion + orthostatic hypotension (hold if NPO/surgery for 24-72h)
Euglycemic DKA (T2DM; even near-normal glucose)
Fournier gangrene (rare necrotizing fasciitis; stop drug, surgery)
Amputation risk: canagliflozin (not class effect)
Bone fractures: canagliflozin
Benefits outweigh risks in most high-risk patients

CV benefit proven: liraglutide (LEADER), semaglutide SC (SUSTAIN-6), dulaglutide (REWIND), albiglutide
Weight loss: semaglutide > liraglutide > dulaglutide > exenatide
Frequency: exenatide BID; liraglutide daily; semaglutide/dulaglutide weekly
Adverse effects: nausea/vomiting (most common; dose-related; titrate slowly), pancreatitis (rare)
Contraindications: personal/family history MTC or MEN2; pregnancy

1. Fluids: 0.9% NaCl 1L/h initially; switch to 0.45% NaCl with dextrose when glucose <250
2. Potassium: replace to >=3.5 BEFORE starting insulin; add 20-40 mEq/L to fluids if K+ 3.5-5.5
3. Insulin: 0.1 unit/kg/h regular (hold until K+ >=3.5)
4. DKA resolution: glucose <200, bicarb >=15, pH >=7.3, anion gap normal
5. Bicarb: only if pH <6.9 (controversial)
6. Transition to SQ insulin 1-2h before stopping infusion

Mechanism: activates AMPK; reduces hepatic gluconeogenesis; improves insulin sensitivity
Benefits: weight-neutral/modest loss; no hypoglycemia alone; CV benefit (UKPDS); first-line T2DM
Contraindications: CrCl <30 (lactic acidosis risk); hepatic impairment; excessive alcohol
Hold: 48h before contrast if eGFR 30-60 (restart after recheck); acute illness
Adverse effects: GI (take with food; ER formulation better); B12 deficiency (check annually)

Primary hypothyroidism: HIGH TSH, LOW free T4
Primary hyperthyroidism: LOW TSH, HIGH free T4
Secondary hypothyroidism (pituitary): LOW TSH, LOW free T4
Subclinical hypothyroidism: HIGH TSH, NORMAL free T4
Sick euthyroid (NTI): low T3, low-normal T4, low/normal TSH (do NOT treat; reassess after illness)
Amiodarone: increases T4, decreases T3 conversion (can obscure interpretation)

Minor stress (local anesthesia, minor procedure): double home dose on day of procedure only
Moderate stress (knee replacement, open cholecystectomy): hydrocortisone 25-50mg IV day of surgery; taper to home dose over 1-2 days
Major stress (cardiac surgery, septic shock): hydrocortisone 50-100mg q6-8h; taper when hemodynamically stable
Note: do NOT delay stress dosing for testing in suspected adrenal crisis

Oral bisphosphonates: alendronate 70mg weekly, risedronate 35mg weekly (must remain upright 30-60 min after; avoid CrCl <30-35)
IV zoledronic acid 5mg annually (avoid CrCl <35)
Denosumab (RANKL inhibitor): renally safe; give q6 months; rebound fractures if stopped abruptly
Teriparatide/abaloparatide (anabolic): used for severe/treatment-failure cases; max 2 years
Atypical femoral fractures: rare complication of >5 years of bisphosphonate; drug holiday after 5 years in low-risk patients

Stage 1: SCr 1.5-1.9x baseline OR +0.3 mg/dL in 48h; UO <0.5 mL/kg/h x 6-12h
Stage 2: SCr 2-2.9x baseline; UO <0.5 mL/kg/h x >=12h
Stage 3: SCr >=3x baseline OR >=4.0 mg/dL OR RRT; UO <0.3 mL/kg/h x 24h or anuria 12h
Prerenal: FeNa <1%, BUN/Cr >20, concentrated urine (high osmolality)
ATN: FeNa >2%, isosthenuria, muddy brown granular casts

Contrast nephropathy: IV hydration pre/post (0.9% NaCl); iso-osmolar contrast; minimize volume
Aminoglycosides: once-daily dosing; avoid volume depletion; monitor trough
Vancomycin: AUC-guided (not trough-only); avoid concurrent nephrotoxins
NSAIDs: avoid in CKD, HF, volume-depleted patients
Amphotericin B: use liposomal form; aggressive hydration; monitor SCr daily

Metformin: avoid CrCl <30
Gabapentin: dose reduce CrCl <60; supplement after HD
SGLT2i: less glycemic effect eGFR <25-45; renal/CV benefit may persist lower
Fondaparinux: avoid CrCl <30
Enoxaparin: 1mg/kg q24h (not q12h) if CrCl <30
Apixaban in ESRD: 5mg BID (or 2.5mg BID if criteria met)
Dabigatran: avoid CrCl <15; 75mg BID if CrCl 15-30

1. Calcium gluconate 1-2g IV (membrane stabilization; ECG changes -- IMMEDIATE)
2. Insulin 10 units regular + dextrose 50g (shifts K+ into cells; onset 15-30 min)
3. Sodium bicarbonate IV (if metabolic acidosis; works synergistically)
4. Nebulized albuterol 10-20mg (shifts K+ intracellularly; additive with insulin)
5. Patiromer or SPS (Kayexalate) PO (delayed effect; hours to days)
6. Dialysis (definitive; refractory or ESRD)

Hyperkalemia: dietary restriction + patiromer (preferred over Kayexalate for safety)
Metabolic acidosis: NaHCO3 to maintain HCO3 >=22 mEq/L (slows GFR decline)
Hyperphosphatemia: sevelamer carbonate (no calcium absorption; reduces vascular calcification); calcium carbonate (cheap; avoid in vascular calcification)
Secondary hyperPTH: active vitamin D (calcitriol) + cinacalcet (dialysis patients)

Step 1: SABA PRN
Step 2: Low-dose ICS + SABA PRN
Step 3: Low-dose ICS + LABA OR medium-dose ICS
Step 4: Medium-dose ICS + LABA
Step 5: High-dose ICS + LABA +/- LAMA (tiotropium)
Step 6: Step 5 + oral corticosteroids
LABA ALONE contraindicated in asthma (increased asthma-related mortality risk)

front: COPD GOLD groups and initial treatment
back:
Group A: bronchodilator PRN (SABA or SAMA)
Group B: LAMA or LABA preferred (add second if very symptomatic)
Group E: LAMA + LABA; add ICS if eos >=300 OR >=2 exacerbations on dual therapy
Add roflumilast (PDE4 inhibitor) for FEV1 <50% + chronic bronchitis + >=2 exacerbations
ICS monotherapy NOT appropriate for COPD

Bronchodilators: SABA + ipratropium nebulized
Steroids: prednisone 40mg daily x 5 days (REDUCE trial)
Antibiotics (if 2/3 Anthonisen criteria: dyspnea + sputum volume + purulence):
- Mild/moderate: azithromycin or doxycycline
- Severe/hospitalized: respiratory FQ or amox-clav
Oxygen: target SpO2 88-92% (avoid hyperoxia)
NIV (BiPAP): if pH <7.35 and PaCO2 elevated (reduces intubation risk)

front: Adult immunization key vaccines and ages
back:
All adults annually: Influenza, COVID-19 booster
Age >=19: Tdap (once), then Td q10 years; HepB series (all adults through 59)
Age >=50: Shingrix (RZV) 2 doses 2-6 months apart
Age >=65: PCV20 alone OR PCV15 then PPSV23 at 1 year; RSV vaccine (Abrysvo or Mresvia; shared decision)
Immunocompromised: live vaccines (MMR, varicella) require CD4 >200 (HIV)

Neuropathic pain/fibromyalgia: SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine) or TCAs
Insomnia: mirtazapine, trazodone
Obesity: bupropion (modest weight loss)
Sexual dysfunction: bupropion or mirtazapine (lowest sexual side effects)
Cardiovascular disease: SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram); avoid TCAs (QT prolongation, orthostasis)
Pregnancy: SSRIs generally safe; avoid paroxetine in 1st trimester (cardiac defects)
Smoking cessation: bupropion SR or varenicline

Therapeutic range: 0.6-1.2 mEq/L (maintenance); 0.8-1.2 (acute mania)
Monitoring: lithium level, SCr/eGFR, TFTs q3-6 months; CBC baseline
Toxicity: fine tremor (therapeutic), coarse tremor/ataxia/confusion (toxic), polyuria
Nephrogenic DI: long-term use; treat with amiloride
Drug interactions raising lithium levels: NSAIDs, thiazides, ACEi/ARBs
Narrow therapeutic index: hydration is critical (dehydration increases levels)

Acute dystonia (hours-days): diphenhydramine 25-50mg IV/IM or benztropine 1-2mg IM
Akathisia (subjective restlessness, days-weeks): propranolol 10-20mg TID, or lower antipsychotic dose
Tardive dyskinesia (months-years of use): switch to clozapine or quetiapine; valbenazine or deutetrabenazine (VMAT2 inhibitors)
Pseudoparkinsonism: dose reduce or switch to low-D2 drug
Highest EPS risk: haloperidol, fluphenazine; Lowest: clozapine, quetiapine

SEROTONIN SYNDROME:
Cause: serotonergic excess (SSRI + MAOI, linezolid + SSRI, triptans + SSRIs)
Onset: rapid (hours)
Findings: agitation, clonus, hyperreflexia, diarrhea, mydriasis
Treatment: cyproheptadine, benzodiazepines, discontinue offending agents
NMS:
Cause: dopamine blockade (antipsychotics)
Onset: days-weeks
Findings: hyperthermia, lead-pipe rigidity, altered MS, high CK, autonomic instability
Treatment: bromocriptine, dantrolene, discontinue antipsychotic

Window: ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours of onset (or last known well)
Key inclusion: measurable neurologic deficit; adult patients
Key exclusions: hemorrhage on CT, anticoagulants (INR >1.7 or DOAC), platelet <100k, glucose <50 or >400, SBP >185/DBP >110 (treat first), recent major surgery <3 months
Extended 3-4.5h: additional exclusions (age >80, NIHSS >25, prior stroke + DM)
Goal: door-to-needle <60 minutes

Focal seizures: levetiracetam, lamotrigine, oxcarbazepine, carbamazepine
Generalized tonic-clonic: valproate (most effective, teratogenic), levetiracetam, lamotrigine
Absence: ethosuximide (first-line), valproate; NOT carbamazepine/phenytoin (worsen absence)
Myoclonic: valproate, levetiracetam, clonazepam
Status epilepticus: IV benzodiazepines FIRST (lorazepam preferred), then fosphenytoin/valproate/levetiracetam

First-line for motor symptoms: carbidopa/levodopa (most effective)
Initial therapy in YOUNG patients (<65): dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) to delay levodopa motor fluctuations
Levodopa complications: wearing-off, ON-OFF fluctuations, dyskinesias (reduce dose; add amantadine for dyskinesias)
AVOID anticholinergics in elderly (trihexyphenidyl, benztropine) -- cognitive side effects
MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline): mild symptomatic benefit; possible neuroprotective effect

PREFERRED:
Clarithromycin triple (if local resistance <15% and no prior macrolide): PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin x 14 days
Bismuth quadruple (PCN allergy or macrolide resistance): PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline x 14 days

CONFIRM ERADICATION: urea breath test or stool antigen >=4 weeks after therapy
(Turn off PPI for 2 weeks before testing)

front: IBD: Crohn vs UC treatment approach
back:
UC (mild-moderate): 5-ASA oral + rectal (mesalamine)
UC (moderate-severe): corticosteroids (induction); azathioprine/6-MP or biologics (maintenance)
Crohn (mild-moderate): budesonide (ileocolonic), 5-ASA (some benefit), metronidazole (perianal)
Crohn (moderate-severe): corticosteroids, then azathioprine/6-MP + anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab)
Biologics: anti-TNF, vedolizumab (gut-selective), ustekinumab (IL-12/23)
Check TPMT before azathioprine/6-MP

ACUTE: lactulose 30-45mL q1h until 2-3 soft bowel movements per day (traps NH3 as NH4+)
SECONDARY PREVENTION: rifaximin 550mg BID + lactulose (reduce recurrence by ~57%)
Precipitants to address: GI bleed, infection, constipation, electrolyte disturbance, medications (BZDs, opioids, diuretics), dietary protein excess
Zinc supplementation: adjunctive
AVOID: benzodiazepines (worsen HE), nephrotoxic drugs

front: Beers Criteria most important PIMs in elderly
back:
HIGH ANTICHOLINERGIC BURDEN: diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, amitriptyline, hydroxyzine, chlorpheniramine -- delirium, falls, urinary retention
BENZODIAZEPINES (all): falls, cognitive impairment, dependence
NSAIDs: GI bleed, AKI, fluid retention -- use acetaminophen instead
LONG-ACTING SULFONYLUREAS: glyburide -- prolonged hypoglycemia
DIGOXIN >0.125mg/day: toxicity; narrow TI in elderly
ANTIPSYCHOTICS (first-gen): EPS, QT prolongation

DEPRESCRIBE/MINIMIZE:
- Benzodiazepines (highest risk)
- Sedative-hypnotics (zolpidem, eszopiclone)
- Anticholinergics
- Antipsychotics
- Opioids
- Alpha-1 blockers (orthostatic hypotension)
- Antiepileptics
ALTERNATIVES:
- Sleep: melatonin, trazodone
- Anxiety: SSRIs, SNRIs
Vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day (reduces falls + fractures)

Sensitivity (SN-N-out): proportion of true positives correctly detected
- High = rules out if NEGATIVE (snout)
- Use for SCREENING (HIV Ab test, troponin series)
Specificity (SP-P-in): proportion of true negatives correctly identified
- High = rules in if POSITIVE (spin)
- Use for CONFIRMATION (RIBA for HCV, Western blot for HIV)
PPV/NPV depend on disease prevalence in that population

ARR = Control event rate - Treatment event rate
NNT = 1 / ARR
NNH = 1 / ARI (absolute risk increase)
RRR = ARR / Control rate (looks more impressive; misleading without context)
Example: Mortality 12% placebo vs 7% treatment
ARR = 5% = 0.05
NNT = 1/0.05 = 20
RRR = 5/12 = 42%
Always report ARR/NNT with RRR for full picture

front: Study design: when to use RR vs OR
back:
RCT and cohort studies: use RELATIVE RISK (RR)
Case-control studies: use ODDS RATIO (OR) (cannot calculate incidence rates in case-control)
OR approximates RR when disease is rare (<10%)
OR always overestimates RR if outcome is common
Hazard Ratio (HR): time-to-event outcomes in survival analysis (RCTs with censoring)
Incidence Rate Ratio (IRR): Poisson regression for count outcomes

ITT: all randomized patients analyzed in assigned groups; regardless of adherence or dropout; GOLD STANDARD for superiority trials; preserves randomization benefits; most conservative estimate
PP: only patients who completed protocol; can overestimate efficacy; used as sensitivity analysis
As-treated: analyzed based on actual treatment received
ITT preferred for: regulatory submissions, pragmatic trials
PP preferred for: non-inferiority trials (ITT can bias toward null, making non-inferior appear equivalent)

front: Types of bias in clinical research
back:
Selection bias: non-random group allocation
Information/recall bias: errors in data collection (retrospective studies)
Confounding: third variable associated with both exposure and outcome
Attrition bias: differential dropout between groups
Publication bias: positive results over-represented in literature (funnel plot asymmetry)
Performance bias: differences in co-interventions between groups
Randomization prevents confounding and selection bias

Process: comparing current medication list to all orders; identifying discrepancies
High-risk transitions: admission, ICU transfer, discharge to SNF/home
Most errors occur at DISCHARGE (unintentional omissions most common)
Best-possible medication history (BPMH): use multiple sources (patient, caregiver, pharmacy, PCP records)
Pharmacist-led reconciliation reduces ADEs by 15-30%
TJC requirement: medication reconciliation required at all care transitions

REMS = Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies: FDA-required programs for high-risk drugs
CLOZAPINE REMS: ANC monitoring required (agranulocytosis risk); no dispensing without ANC check
iPLEDGE: isotretinoin; two forms of contraception required; monthly pregnancy test
THALOMID REMS: thalidomide; teratogenicity; contraception, pregnancy testing
RESTORED MEDS: transmucosal fentanyl products; TIRF REMS access; cancer breakthrough pain only
Safety pharmacies: must enroll in program to dispense

WARM + WET: adequate perfusion, volume overloaded -- IV diuresis (furosemide 2.5x oral dose)
WARM + DRY: compensated (no treatment needed or gentle oral diuresis)
COLD + WET: low perfusion + congestion -- vasopressors + diuresis
COLD + DRY: cardiogenic shock + hypovolemia -- cautious fluid challenge, then vasopressors
Diuretic resistance: use thiazide + loop combination; SGLT2i; consider ultrafiltration

Uses: rate control in AFib; HF symptom relief (no mortality benefit; reduces hospitalizations)
Therapeutic range: 0.5-0.9 ng/mL in HF (lower is better; toxicity >2 ng/mL)
Renal clearance: reduce dose in CKD; hold in AKI
Toxicity: N/V, yellow-green vision (xanthopsia), bradycardia, heart block, any arrhythmia
Electrolytes: hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia increase toxicity risk
Reversal: Digibind (digoxin-specific antibody fragments) for life-threatening toxicity

front: Long QT: key drug classes and torsades de pointes management
back:
Drug classes causing QT prolongation: antipsychotics (haloperidol, ziprasidone), macrolides (azithromycin), fluoroquinolones (moxifloxacin), antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, sotalol, dofetilide), antiemetics (ondansetron), methadone, hydroxychloroquine
Risk increased by: hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, bradycardia
Torsades: IV Mg 2g (first-line even if Mg normal); overdrive pacing or isoproterenol; STOP offending drugs

front: Direct renin inhibitor and neprilysin inhibitor
back:
Aliskiren (direct renin inhibitor): rarely used; combined with ACEi or ARB CONTRAINDICATED in diabetics and CrCl <60
Sacubitril (neprilysin inhibitor): blocks neprilysin (prevents BNP/ANP breakdown); always combined with valsartan (ARNI)
Neprilysin also degrades bradykinin: thus ARNI + ACEi = angioedema risk
ARNI reduces hospitalization and mortality in HFrEF more than enalapril (PARADIGM-HF)
ARNI also benefits HFpEF (PARAGON-HF; reduced hospitalizations in subgroup)

Brugada syndrome: sodium channelopathy; right bundle branch block + ST elevation V1-V3; risk of sudden death
Triggered by: fever, cocaine, TCAs, class IA antiarrhythmics, flecainide
ICD indicated for symptomatic patients
AVOID: sodium channel blockers (flecainide, propafenone, TCAs), fever (treat aggressively)
Prevention of VF: quinidine (off-label); isoproterenol in acute VF storm

Oropharyngeal candidiasis: fluconazole 200mg x 7-14 days OR nystatin swish-and-swallow
Esophageal candidiasis: fluconazole 200-400mg x 14-21 days (nystatin NOT effective)
Candidemia: echinocandin first-line x 14 days after last positive blood culture
Invasive Aspergillosis: voriconazole (first-line) OR isavuconazole
Cryptococcal meningitis: AmB + flucytosine x 2 weeks induction, then fluconazole

Most common: E. coli (80%), Klebsiella, Staphylococcus saprophyticus (young women)
Uncomplicated UTI: nitrofurantoin 100mg ER BID x 5 days OR TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days
AVOID: fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated (resistance stewardship)
Complicated UTI/pyelonephritis: FQ (cipro/levo) x 5-7 days OR ceftriaxone IV
Catheter-associated UTI (CAUTI): treat only if symptomatic; remove/replace catheter; avoid prophylactic antibiotics

De-escalation: narrow spectrum when culture data available
Duration: use shortest effective duration (5-7 days CAP; 5 days uncomplicated UTI; 4-6 weeks osteomyelitis)
Prospective audit and feedback: most effective stewardship intervention
Prior authorization: pre-approval for restricted antibiotics
Biomarkers: PCT to guide de-escalation in CAP/sepsis
Formulary restriction: limit broad-spectrum agents (carbapenems, colistin) to preserve resistance patterns

Lyme disease early: doxycycline 100mg BID x 10-21 days (also prevents co-infections)
Lyme disseminated/neuro/cardiac: doxycycline or IV ceftriaxone
Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (RMSF): doxycycline is drug of choice (even in children -- rash plus fever, treat empirically)
Ehrlichiosis/Anaplasmosis: doxycycline
Babesiosis: atovaquone + azithromycin (mild-moderate); clindamycin + quinine (severe)
Doxycycline: covers all major tick-borne illnesses except babesiosis

Hepatitis B: tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) or entecavir (first-line); pegylated interferon (finite duration, selected patients)
Goal HBV: HBsAg loss, HBV DNA undetectable
Hepatitis C: direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) -- pan-genotypic glecaprevir/pibrentasvir 8 weeks (no cirrhosis) OR sofosbuvir/velpatasvir 12 weeks
SVR12 = cure (>95% with DAAs)
Hep A/E: supportive only
HIV/HBV co-infection: include HBV-active antiretrovirals (TAF/FTC or TDF/FTC) in HIV regimen

Highly protein-bound drugs (>90%): warfarin, phenytoin, valproate, NSAIDs, furosemide, amiodarone
Free drug = pharmacologically active fraction
Displacement interactions: when two highly protein-bound drugs compete, free drug levels increase transiently (usually self-limited by redistribution + elimination)
Hypoalbuminemia: increases free fraction for protein-bound drugs (warfarin, phenytoin, valproate)
Acidic drugs bind albumin; Basic drugs bind alpha-1 acid glycoprotein

front: Hepatic clearance: first-pass and extraction ratio
back:
High extraction ratio drugs (>0.7): extensive first-pass metabolism; oral bioavailability low; elimination is blood-flow dependent
Examples: morphine, propranolol, lidocaine, verapamil, nitroglycerin
Low extraction ratio drugs (<0.3): enzyme-capacity limited; affected by enzyme inducers/inhibitors; protein binding changes matter
Examples: warfarin, phenytoin, caffeine, diazepam
Hepatic disease: reduces first-pass (increases bioavailability of high-extraction drugs) AND reduces CL (increases t1/2)

front: Therapeutic drug monitoring: which drugs and why
back:
Narrow therapeutic index drugs requiring TDM:
- Vancomycin: AUC-guided (nephrotoxicity + efficacy)
- Aminoglycosides: Cmax/MIC (efficacy) + trough (toxicity)
- Phenytoin: nonlinear PK; hypoalbuminemia correction
- Digoxin: narrow TI; level 0.5-0.9 ng/mL
- Lithium: narrow TI; 0.6-1.2 mEq/L
- Cyclosporine/tacrolimus: narrow TI; many drug interactions
- Phenobarbital: 15-40 mcg/mL

GFR-dependent drugs: most antibiotics, metformin, gabapentin, DOACs (especially dabigatran)
Dose adjustment approaches:
1. Extend dosing interval (maintain same dose) -- gentamicin extended interval
2. Reduce dose (maintain same interval) -- vancomycin dose reduction in severe CKD
3. Combination -- reduce dose AND extend interval
Dialysis: removes drugs with small Vd + high water solubility + low protein binding; supplement dose post-HD

front: Insulin types and duration of action
back:
Rapid-acting (onset 15 min; peak 1-2h; duration 3-5h): lispro, aspart, glulisine (take with meals)
Short-acting regular (onset 30-60 min; peak 2-4h; duration 6-8h): can be used IV in DKA
Intermediate (NPH): onset 2-4h; peak 4-10h; duration 10-16h
Long-acting (basal; flat profile, 24h): glargine (U-100, U-300), detemir, degludec (U-100, U-200)
Ultra-long: insulin degludec (42 hours)
Insulin glargine U-300 vs U-100: more concentrated; cannot mix; less nocturnal hypoglycemia

front: Thyroid cancer and amiodarone thyroid effects
back:
Papillary thyroid cancer (most common; best prognosis): treatment = surgery + RAI + levothyroxine suppression
Anaplastic (most aggressive): chemotherapy + radiation; poor prognosis
Medullary (MTC): associated with MEN2; RET proto-oncogene mutation; calcitonin biomarker; GLP-1 RAs CONTRAINDICATED with MTC history
Amiodarone: high iodine content (37%); blocks T4 to T3 conversion; causes both hypo and hyperthyroidism; half-life 58 days

Hyperparathyroidism: high PTH + high calcium + low phosphate
Causes: primary (adenoma most common); secondary (CKD, vit D deficiency); tertiary (autonomous after secondary)
Hypercalcemia sx: 'bones, stones, groans, moans' (bone pain, kidney stones, constipation, depression)
Hypoparathyroidism: low PTH + low calcium + high phosphate
Causes: post-surgical, autoimmune
Treatment: IV calcium gluconate (acute) or calcium carbonate + active vitamin D (calcitriol)

Primary treatment: lifestyle modification (weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and ovulation)
Menstrual irregularity/hirsutism: combined oral contraceptives (first-line)
Insulin resistance/metabolic: metformin
Fertility: clomiphene citrate (first-line for ovulation induction), letrozole (preferred per some guidelines)
Anti-androgens (for hirsutism): spironolactone (use with OCP for contraception)
Excess androgens: finasteride (5-alpha reductase inhibitor) -- second-line for hirsutism

Normal: <30 mg/day (<30 mg/g albumin-creatinine ratio)
Microalbuminuria (now called 'moderately increased albuminuria'): 30-300 mg/g ACR
Macroalbuminuria ('severely increased'): >300 mg/g ACR
Nephrotic range: >3.5 g/day (or >3500 mg/g) -- hypoalbuminemia, edema, lipiduria, hypercoagulability
Significance: each doubling of proteinuria accelerates CKD progression; ACEi/ARB reduce proteinuria and slow progression

front: Acute tubular necrosis (ATN) causes and management
back:
Ischemic ATN: prolonged hypoperfusion (shock, cardiac surgery, contrast)
Nephrotoxic ATN: aminoglycosides, vancomycin, contrast, cisplatin, amphotericin B, myoglobin (rhabdomyolysis)
Lab findings: FeNa >2%, isosthenuria (urine Osm ~300), muddy brown granular casts
Management: supportive (correct volume, avoid nephrotoxins, dose adjust drugs)
Rhabdomyolysis: aggressive IV fluids (target UO 200-300 mL/h), sodium bicarbonate (controversial)
Avoid nephrotoxin combinations: vancomycin + pip-tazo (higher AKI risk vs other combos)

Definition: SCr increase >=0.5 mg/dL or >=25% within 48-72h after contrast
High-risk: pre-existing CKD (eGFR <60), DM, dehydration, high contrast volume
Prevention:
1. IV hydration: 0.9% NaCl 1 mL/kg/h 6-12h pre/post
2. Use iso-osmolar or low-osmolar contrast
3. Minimize contrast volume
4. Hold nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, metformin if eGFR 30-60 for 48h post)
5. N-acetylcysteine: controversial; low risk; often given 600mg BID around procedure

Berlin definition (2012):
- Timing: within 1 week of clinical insult
- Bilateral infiltrates (not fully explained by effusions, collapse)
- PaO2/FiO2 <300 with PEEP >=5
Severity: Mild 200-300, Moderate 100-200, Severe <100
Management: ARDSNet protocol (TV 6 mL/kg IBW, Pplat <=30)
Prone positioning: >16h/day for severe ARDS (PROSEVA trial)
Neuromuscular blockade: controversial; used for severe ARDS with ventilator dyssynchrony

pRBC transfusion threshold:
- Stable non-cardiac ICU: Hgb <7 g/dL (TRICC, TRISS trials)
- Cardiac surgery, ACS, HF: Hgb <8-9 g/dL (liberal threshold)
- Massive transfusion: 1:1:1 ratio (pRBC:FFP:platelets)
FFP: warfarin reversal with bleeding, massive transfusion, Factor V/X deficiency
Platelets: <10k (prophylaxis), <50k with bleeding/pre-procedure, <100k with CNS bleeding
Cryoprecipitate: fibrinogen <100 mg/dL, von Willebrand disease, hemophilia A

PAIN: assess with NRS (0-10) or CPOT (non-verbal); opioids for pain (fentanyl IV preferred)
AGITATION: target RASS -1 to 0 (light sedation); propofol or dexmedetomidine preferred over BZDs
DELIRIUM: CAM-ICU or ICDSC; A-F bundle (Assess pain, Both SAT+SBT, Choice of sedation, Delirium monitoring, Early mobility, Family engagement)
BZDs increase delirium duration; dexmedetomidine reduces duration vs midazolam (MENDS)
Dexmedetomidine: causes bradycardia and hypotension; does not cause respiratory depression

Initiate EN within 24-48h of ICU admission (if hemodynamically stable and GI tract functional)
Caloric target: 25-30 kcal/kg/day (avoid overfeeding; hypocaloric/trophic feeding first 1 week for obese)
Protein: 1.2-2.0 g/kg/day (IBW in obese)
Contral PN: avoid unless EN not feasible after 7 days (increases infection risk)
Glucose target: 140-180 mg/dL (tight control <110 increases hypoglycemia and mortality - NICE-SUGAR)
Prokinetics (metoclopramide, erythromycin) for high gastric residuals

Types:
- Hyperactive: agitation, pulling lines, combative
- Hypoactive: withdrawn, confused, quiet (most common; most missed)
- Mixed: fluctuates between both
Risk factors: age >65, dementia, prior delirium, infection, surgery, immobility, sleep deprivation, medications (anticholinergics, BZDs, opioids), pain, dehydration, sensory impairment
Screening: CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) -- acute onset + inattention required
Management: non-pharmacologic first (orientation, sleep hygiene, mobilize, minimize drugs)

Preferred: acetaminophen 325-650mg q4-6h (max 3g/day if liver disease or heavy alcohol)
Topical agents: diclofenac gel, lidocaine patches (low systemic absorption)
Avoid: NSAIDs (GI bleed, AKI, CV risk, fluid retention)
Opioids: use only when other options exhausted; start low and go slow; monitor for constipation/falls; use bowel regimen prophylactically
Neuropathic pain: duloxetine or pregabalin (preferred over amitriptyline in elderly)
Gabapentin: effective but dose reduce in CKD; sedation risk

ACUTE MANIA: lithium + antipsychotic OR valproate + antipsychotic; benzodiazepines for agitation
ACUTE BIPOLAR DEPRESSION: quetiapine (FDA-approved), lamotrigine (slower onset), lurasidone
Antidepressant MONOTHERAPY: AVOID (triggers mania); if used, combine with mood stabilizer
MAINTENANCE: lithium (best evidence for suicide prevention), valproate, lamotrigine (depression prevention), quetiapine
CYCLOTHYMIA: lithium or valproate
Pregnancy: lithium (Ebstein anomaly risk, but low absolute risk); avoid valproate (teratogen)

ALCOHOL USE DISORDER:
- Detox: benzodiazepines (CIWA protocol)
- Maintenance: naltrexone (reduces cravings; opioid antagonist), acamprosate (reduces withdrawal symptoms), disulfiram (aversion; monitor compliance)
OPIOID USE DISORDER:
- Methadone (daily observed; opioid agonist; QT monitoring)
- Buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone; partial agonist; office-based)
- Naltrexone XR (Vivitrol; monthly injection; require full opioid detox first)
NICOTINE: varenicline (most effective), bupropion SR, NRT (patch, gum, lozenge)

Indications: treatment-resistant schizophrenia (2+ failed antipsychotics); reduction of suicidality in schizophrenia; tardive dyskinesia
Black box warnings: AGRANULOCYTOSIS (ANC monitoring required; CLOZAPINE REMS program), SEIZURES, MYOCARDITIS, ORTHOSTASIS
ANC monitoring: weekly x 26 weeks, biweekly x 26 weeks, then monthly
Other ADEs: metabolic syndrome, sedation, hypersalivation, constipation, seizures (dose-dependent)
No EPS or tardive dyskinesia (lowest D2 binding of all antipsychotics)

HIGH emetogenic chemotherapy (cisplatin, doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide): triple antiemetic: 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone + NK1 antagonist (aprepitant or fosaprepitant)
Add olanzapine 10mg as 4th agent for highly refractory CINV
MODERATE emetogenic (carbo, irinotecan): 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone
LOW/MINIMAL emetogenic: prochlorperazine or metoclopramide PRN
Delayed CINV (>24h post chemo): dexamethasone, aprepitant
Anticipatory CINV (conditioned response): lorazepam, behavioral therapy

front: Tumor lysis syndrome: prevention and treatment
back:
TLS: rapid cell death releasing K+, phosphate, uric acid, causing AKI, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia
High risk: lymphoma, leukemia, bulky disease before chemotherapy
Prevention: allopurinol (reduces new uric acid production), hydration, rasburicase (high-risk patients; rapidly eliminates existing uric acid)
Rasburicase contraindicated: G6PD deficiency (causes hemolytic anemia)
Treatment of established TLS: aggressive hydration + rasburicase + electrolyte management + consider dialysis

Step 1 (mild pain, VAS 1-3): non-opioid -- acetaminophen, NSAIDs
Step 2 (moderate pain, VAS 4-6): weak opioid -- tramadol, codeine
Step 3 (severe pain, VAS 7-10): strong opioid -- morphine, hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl
Principles: by mouth (oral preferred), by the clock (scheduled not PRN), by the ladder
Adjuvants at any step: antidepressants (neuropathic), anticonvulsants, bisphosphonates (bone pain), corticosteroids
Bowel regimen ALWAYS with opioids (no tolerance to constipation)

No specific antibiotic without source identification
Fever workup: <28 days = full sepsis workup + IV antibiotics; 29-60 days = stratify by risk (Rochester criteria)
Antipyretics: acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg q4-6h OR ibuprofen 5-10 mg/kg q6-8h (>=6 months only)
AVOID: aspirin in children (Reye syndrome risk)
Febrile seizure: typically benign; no antiepileptic prophylaxis needed for simple febrile seizures
Meningitis empiric: cefotaxime or ceftriaxone (+ vancomycin) + dexamethasone for children >1 month

Palivizumab (monoclonal antibody): monthly IM injections during RSV season (November-March)
Indications (AAP 2014): premature infants (<29 weeks) in first year; hemodynamically significant CHD; CLD (BPD) requiring treatment in first 2 years; immunocompromised
Do NOT give if: otherwise healthy infants >28 weeks without CHD/CLD
Nirsevimab (Beyfortus 2023): single dose; newer; broader indication for ALL infants <8 months entering first RSV season (has largely replaced palivizumab for most indications)
Duration: palivizumab given up to 5 doses; nirsevimab is single dose

Diagnosis: middle ear effusion + acute onset of symptoms (ear pain, fever, bulging membrane)
AOME (acute otitis media): requires antibiotic in <2 years or severe cases
Watchful waiting: >=2 years with non-severe AOM (use pain management; prescribe antibiotics only if no improvement in 48-72h)
First-line: amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day divided BID (high dose overcomes resistant S. pneumoniae)
Penicillin allergy: azithromycin (3-day course) or cefdinir
Treatment failure: amoxicillin-clavulanate (covers beta-lactamase producing H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis)

95% CI: range of values in which the true population parameter falls 95% of the time with repeated sampling
Statistical significance: CI for RR/OR/HR does NOT include 1.0 (null)
CI for mean difference does NOT include 0.0 (null)
Wider CI: less precision (smaller sample, higher variability)
Narrower CI: more precision (larger sample, lower variability)
Confidence interval vs p-value: CI provides magnitude + direction of effect; p-value only indicates significance

front: Types of error in hypothesis testing
back:
Type I error (alpha): false positive -- rejecting H0 when true; probability = alpha (usually 0.05)
Type II error (beta): false negative -- failing to reject H0 when false; probability = beta (usually 0.20)
Power = 1 - beta = ability to detect a true difference (usually 0.80 = 80%)
Small sample sizes increase Type II errors (underpowered studies)
Multiple comparisons increase Type I errors (use Bonferroni correction)
Chance, bias, confounding must all be addressed before causality inferred

Superiority trial: H0 is that treatments are equal; aims to show treatment A is BETTER than B
Non-inferiority trial: aims to show new treatment is NOT WORSE than standard by more than delta (non-inferiority margin)
Non-inferiority margin: clinically acceptable threshold; set a priori; should be smaller than treatment effect of control
ITT analysis biases toward null (no difference) in superiority; PP analysis is preferred sensitivity analysis in NI
Switching endpoints: if NI is met, can then test for superiority (hierarchical testing)

Beta-blockers: first-line for symptom control (reduce HR and O2 demand)
CCBs: second-line if beta-blocker CI; also for vasospastic/Prinzmetal angina
Long-acting nitrates: as-needed or scheduled for exertional angina
Ranolazine: add-on for refractory angina (late Na current blocker; no hemodynamic effects)
Ivabradine: used if beta-blocker CI (pure HR reduction)
Revascularization: for persistent symptoms or high-risk anatomy

First-line: aspirin 750-1000mg TID or ibuprofen 600mg TID x 1-2 weeks (+ PPI)
Add colchicine 0.5mg BID x 3 months (reduces recurrence -- COPE, ICAP trials)
Avoid: corticosteroids as first-line (increase recurrence rate)
Corticosteroids reserved for: NSAID contraindications, specific causes (lupus, uremia)
Recurrent pericarditis: repeat colchicine; IL-1 antagonist (anakinra) for refractory cases
Monitor: CRP normalization guides duration of therapy

front: Tachyarrhythmia pharmacotherapy: rate vs rhythm control
back:
RATE CONTROL (AFib): beta-blockers (metoprolol, carvedilol), non-DHP CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil); digoxin (add-on for sedentary patients)
AVOID non-DHP CCBs in HFrEF (negative inotropes)
RHYTHM CONTROL AGENTS:
- Class IA: quinidine (VT, AFib; high side effect profile)
- Class IC: flecainide, propafenone (AFib, SVT; AVOID if structural heart disease)
- Class III: amiodarone (most effective; highest toxicity), sotalol, dofetilide (QT monitoring), dronedarone (avoid in HF)

Mechanism: indirect factor Xa inhibitor (via antithrombin); synthetic pentasaccharide
Does NOT bind heparin-PF4 antibodies -- SAFE in HIT
Renal clearance: avoid CrCl <30
No antidote (protamine does NOT reverse)
Dose: 2.5mg SQ daily (VTE prophylaxis); 5-10mg SQ daily (VTE treatment; weight-based)
Advantage over LMWH: no HIT risk; disadvantage: no antidote, renal restriction, cannot use in ACS PCI

UFH: protamine sulfate (1mg per 100 units UFH; max 50mg; rapid infusion causes hypotension)
LMWH: protamine partially reverses (60-80%); anti-Xa activity remains
Fondaparinux: NO reversal agent (use rFVIIa off-label in life-threatening bleeding)
Bivalirudin: short half-life (25 min); reverses on its own; no specific antidote
Argatroban: hepatically metabolized; short half-life; no reversal agent

front: Drug-resistant bacteria: key organisms and treatment
back:
MRSA: vancomycin (AUC-guided) OR daptomycin (bacteremia) OR linezolid (pneumonia/SSTI)
VRE: linezolid OR daptomycin
ESBL-producing organisms: carbapenems (definitive); avoid cephalosporins/pip-tazo
Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE): ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, colistin (last resort)
Pseudomonas aeruginosa MDR: ceftolozane-tazobactam, ceftazidime-avibactam, colistin

Aminoglycosides: nephrotoxicity (tubular) + ototoxicity (cochlear and vestibular; irreversible)
Vancomycin: nephrotoxicity (synergistic with aminoglycosides), Red man syndrome (not allergy; slow infusion)
Fluoroquinolones: QT prolongation, tendinopathy (Achilles rupture), peripheral neuropathy, C. difficile
Linezolid: serotonin syndrome (MAOI effect; avoid with SSRIs), myelosuppression (>2 weeks)
Chlorampenicol: aplastic anemia, Gray baby syndrome (neonates)
Tetracyclines: photosensitivity, dental/bone development (children, pregnancy)

Prophylaxis:
- Chloroquine-sensitive areas: chloroquine 500mg weekly
- Chloroquine-resistant areas: atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone) daily OR mefloquine weekly OR doxycycline daily
Treatment (uncomplicated P. falciparum):
- Artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT): artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem)
Severe/cerebral malaria:
- IV artesunate first-line (replaces IV quinidine in USA)
P. vivax/ovale: add primaquine (prevents relapse; test G6PD first)

Pharmacokinetic (ADME-based):
- Absorption: antacids chelate tetracyclines/FQs; food effects
- Distribution: protein displacement (minor clinical significance)
- Metabolism: CYP enzyme induction/inhibition (most common clinically significant)
- Elimination: competition for renal tubular secretion (e.g., probenecid + penicillin)
Pharmacodynamic:
- Additive: two drugs with same mechanism
- Synergistic: greater than additive (e.g., AmB + flucytosine)
- Antagonistic: two drugs with opposing mechanisms

front: Non-renal drug elimination: hepatic and biliary
back:
Hepatic metabolism: CYP450 (phase I: oxidation), glucuronidation/sulfation (phase II: conjugation)
Biliary excretion: high MW compounds; drugs with large MW and polar conjugates (e.g., ceftriaxone, rifampin, erythromycin)
Enterohepatic recirculation: drug excreted in bile, reabsorbed in gut; prolongs effect (e.g., oral contraceptives, phenobarbital)
Hepatic impairment: reduce dose of high-extraction drugs; avoid hepatotoxic drugs in liver disease
Child-Pugh score (A/B/C) or MELD score guides dosing adjustments in hepatic disease

front: Contraception: hormonal options and medical considerations
back:
Combined OCP (estrogen + progestin): highly effective; AVOID in: active DVT/PE, ischemic stroke, MI, migraines with aura, uncontrolled HTN, breast cancer, age >35 + smoking (WHO 4 = absolutely avoid)
Progestin-only pills (POPs): safe in breastfeeding, HTN, migraines with aura
Levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena): excellent efficacy; reduces menstrual bleeding
Copper IUD: non-hormonal; most effective EC (insert within 5 days); may increase menstrual flow
Depo-Provera (medroxyprogesterone IM): every 13 weeks; delays return of fertility; bone density loss with long-term use

Screening: 1-hour 50g GCT at 24-28 weeks; if glucose >=130-140 mg/dL -- proceed to 3-hour 100g OGTT
Diagnosis (OGTT 3-hour, Carpenter-Coustan criteria): fasting >=95, 1h >=180, 2h >=155, 3h >=140 (2 or more values)
First-line treatment: medical nutrition therapy (MNT) + exercise
If goals not met: insulin (preferred in pregnancy; safety established)
Metformin: used by some guidelines; crosses placenta
A1c target: <6% (first trimester); fasting <95, postprandial <140 (1h) or <120 (2h)

Classic triad: episodic hypertension, headache, diaphoresis
Diagnosis: 24-hour urine catecholamines/metanephrines OR plasma metanephrines (most sensitive)
Treatment: surgical resection
Preoperative preparation: alpha-blocker FIRST (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) x 1-2 weeks to control BP
Then add beta-blocker ONLY after adequate alpha-blockade (adding BB first can cause unopposed alpha -- hypertensive crisis)
Intraoperative: phentolamine (IV alpha-blocker) for hypertensive surges

Nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria >3.5g, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia):
- Children: minimal change disease (steroids); adults: focal segmental glomerulosclerosis
- DM: diabetic nephropathy (most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults)
Nephritic syndrome (hematuria, red cell casts, mild proteinuria, hypertension):
- Post-strep GN: resolves with supportive care
- IgA nephropathy: most common GN worldwide
- Lupus nephritis: ISN/RPS classification; treat with cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate
- ANCA vasculitis: rituximab or cyclophosphamide

Calcium carbonate: inexpensive; increases serum calcium; avoid if hypercalcemia or vascular calcification
Calcium acetate: more elemental phosphorus binding per mg calcium; lower calcium load than carbonate
Sevelamer carbonate/HCl: no calcium or aluminum; reduces vascular calcification (best CV evidence); GI side effects; more expensive
Lanthanum carbonate: effective; chewed with meals; concern for accumulation (minimal clinical evidence)
Sucroferric oxyhydroxide: iron-based; minimal systemic iron absorption; fewest tablets per day

Endothelin receptor antagonists (ERA): bosentan, ambrisentan, macitentan -- reduce vasoconstriction; watch LFTs
Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (PDE5i): sildenafil, tadalafil -- increase cGMP, cause vasodilation
Prostacyclin analogs: epoprostenol (IV; most effective for severe), treprostinil (SQ, IV, inhaled), iloprost (inhaled), selexipag (oral)
SGC stimulators: riociguat (only drug approved for both PAH and CTEPH)
Treatment ladder: risk stratification guides combination therapy
Avoid: beta-blockers (reduce CO), CCBs (only in vasoreactive positive test)

front: Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) and drug interactions
back:
CPAP: first-line treatment for moderate-severe OSA
Drugs that worsen OSA: benzodiazepines, opioids, muscle relaxants, alcohol
Drugs that can help: avoid sedatives; weight loss medications may help
Post-surgery risk: opioids in OSA patients increase respiratory depression risk; use non-opioid analgesics when possible
Modafinil/armodafinil: for residual sleepiness after optimal CPAP use
Solriamfetol: selective dopamine/NE reuptake inhibitor for OSA-associated sleepiness

PPI: IV pantoprazole or esomeprazole (bolus + infusion) before and after endoscopy -- reduces rebleeding
High-dose PPI: 80mg IV bolus + 8mg/h infusion x 72h after endoscopic hemostasis
Somatostatin analogs (octreotide): used for variceal bleeding (reduce portal pressure)
Antibiotics (norfloxacin or ceftriaxone): for cirrhosis patients with GI bleed (reduces bacterial translocation and mortality)
Hold: anticoagulants/antiplatelets until bleeding controlled
Transfusion: target Hgb 7-8 g/dL (restrictive strategy reduces rebleeding and mortality - TRICC-GI)

Anti-TNF agents: infliximab (IV), adalimumab (SQ), certolizumab pegol, golimumab
- Check: LTBI (IGRA/TST), HBsAg before starting (risk reactivation)
Vedolizumab (gut-selective alpha4beta7 integrin blocker): preferred in elderly/immunocompromised (no systemic immunosuppression)
Ustekinumab (IL-12/23 blocker): effective for Crohn; moderate immunosuppression
Tofacitinib (JAK inhibitor): UC; AVOID in patients with CV risk or thromboembolic history
Risankizumab/mirikizumab (IL-23 p19): newer agents for UC and Crohn

STRESS (leakage with cough/sneeze): pelvic floor exercises (Kegel); alpha-agonists (pseudoephedrine off-label); avoid oxybutynin
URGE (OAB -- sudden urge): mirabegron (beta-3 agonist; preferred in elderly; less cognitive effects) OR oxybutynin/tolterodine (avoid extended-release oxybutynin in elderly -- high anticholinergic burden)
OVERFLOW (retention): alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) for BPH; cholinergics (bethanechol)
FUNCTIONAL: address underlying cause (mobility, access)
AVOID: oxybutynin IR in elderly (Beers Criteria; high anticholinergic burden)

Screening: DXA scan
- Women >=65 (universal)
- Postmenopausal women <65 with risk factors
- Men >=70 (ISCD recommendation)
Diagnosis: T-score <=-2.5 (osteoporosis); -1.0 to -2.5 (osteopenia)
Treatment threshold (FRAX): 10-year hip fracture risk >=3% OR major osteoporotic fracture risk >=20%
First-line treatment: alendronate or risedronate (oral) OR zoledronic acid (IV)
Calcium + Vitamin D: 1000-1200 mg/day calcium + 800-2000 IU vitamin D (adjunctive)

STIMULANTS (first-line):
- Methylphenidate (Ritalin, Concerta): dopamine/NE reuptake inhibitor; immediate vs extended release
- Amphetamines (Adderall, Vyvanse): dopamine/NE releaser + reuptake inhibitor; Vyvanse = prodrug (lower abuse potential)
NON-STIMULANTS (second-line):
- Atomoxetine (Strattera): selective NE reuptake inhibitor; no abuse potential; delayed onset
- Clonidine/guanfacine ER: alpha-2 agonists; useful for tics + ADHD or when stimulants CI
Monitor: cardiovascular effects (HR, BP); growth velocity in children

Tapering required to avoid withdrawal (seizures, death)
Withdrawal symptoms: anxiety, tremor, sweating, insomnia, seizures, delirium (can be fatal)
Management:
- Taper long-acting BZD (chlordiazepoxide, diazepam) even if patient was on short-acting
- Phenobarbital: alternative for severe dependence
- CIWA (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment): score-based protocol for symptom-triggered dosing
Alcohol withdrawal similarly treated (BZDs cross-reactive)
Avoid abrupt discontinuation after chronic use (>2-4 weeks at any dose)

Low-moderate efficacy (first-line): interferon beta (1a, 1b), glatiramer acetate
Moderate efficacy: dimethyl fumarate (oral), teriflunomide (oral), fingolimod (oral)
High efficacy (for aggressive MS): natalizumab (IV; JC virus test before -- PML risk), ocrelizumab (anti-CD20; IV), siponimod, alemtuzumab
Acute relapse: IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x 3-5 days (speeds recovery, not long-term outcome)
Progressive MS: ocrelizumab (primary progressive MS; first approved for this)

ACUTE treatment (mild-moderate): NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen), acetaminophen, combination analgesics
ACUTE treatment (moderate-severe): triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan); gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant - CGRP antagonists); ditans (lasmiditan - 5-HT1F)
Avoid triptans in: coronary artery disease, stroke, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic migraine
PREVENTION (if >=4 headache days/month): propranolol, topiramate, amitriptyline, valproate, CGRP antagonists (monoclonal antibodies: erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab)
Calcium supplements may reduce menstrual migraine frequency

Mechanism: autoimmune destruction of acetylcholine receptors at NMJ
First-line symptomatic: pyridostigmine (AChE inhibitor) -- increases ACh at NMJ
Immunosuppression (long-term): prednisone + azathioprine or mycophenolate
Crisis management: IV IVIG or plasmapheresis
Avoid: aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, beta-blockers (worsen NMJ transmission)
Thymectomy: indicated if thymoma present; also improves outcomes in AChR+ patients without thymoma
Anti-MuSK positive MG: rituximab may be more effective than traditional immunosuppression

Imatinib (Gleevec): BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor; CML and GIST
Trastuzumab (Herceptin): anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody; HER2+ breast/gastric cancer; cardiotoxic (EF monitoring)
Rituximab: anti-CD20; B-cell lymphoma/leukemia; reactivates HBV (screen HBsAg first)
Bevacizumab: anti-VEGF; inhibits angiogenesis; risk of hemorrhage and wound healing
Pembrolizumab/nivolumab: PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors; immune-related adverse effects (irAEs): pneumonitis, colitis, endocrinopathies
EGFR inhibitors (erlotinib, gefitinib): NSCLC with EGFR mutations; acneiform rash (sign of efficacy)

CML: imatinib (1st gen TKI), dasatinib/nilotinib (2nd gen), ponatinib (3rd gen for T315I)
AML: cytarabine + daunorubicin (7+3 induction); midostaurin (FLT3+); venetoclax + azacitidine (older/unfit)
ALL: vincristine + prednisone + L-asparaginase backbone; ponatinib/dasatinib for Ph+
CLL: ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor), venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor), obinutuzumab
Multiple Myeloma: bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor), lenalidomide, daratumumab (anti-CD38) + dex
HL: ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine); bleomycin pulmonary toxicity

front: Drug information hierarchy: primary, secondary, tertiary
back:
TERTIARY (synthesized summaries): textbooks, Micromedex, Lexi-Comp, UpToDate, package inserts
- PROS: quick access, pre-evaluated; CONS: may lag current evidence; simplified
SECONDARY (indexing/abstracting): PubMed/MEDLINE, Cochrane, Embase, IPA
- Helps locate primary literature
PRIMARY (original research): RCTs, cohort studies, case reports, clinical guidelines
- GOLD STANDARD for clinical decisions
BCPS Domain 3: use primary literature to evaluate evidence; understand levels of evidence

PDSA cycle: Plan-Do-Study-Act (iterative improvement)
DMAIC (Six Sigma): Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control (eliminate defects)
Lean: eliminate waste; improve efficiency; reduce non-value-added steps
Root cause analysis (RCA): retrospective analysis of sentinel events
FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis): proactive risk assessment
JCAHO/TJC: accreditation; National Patient Safety Goals
Pharmacist roles: medication reconciliation, stewardship, transitions of care, formulary management

Cost-effectiveness analysis (CEA): compares cost per unit of health outcome (e.g., cost per QALY)
QALY (Quality-Adjusted Life Year): 1 QALY = 1 year in perfect health; used to compare interventions
ICER (Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio): (cost of new - cost of old) / (effect of new - effect of old)
Threshold: <$50,000-$100,000/QALY generally considered cost-effective in US
Cost-benefit analysis: converts health outcomes to dollar value
Cost-minimization: compares costs when two therapies have equal efficacy (e.g., generic vs brand)

front: Measures of association: RR, OR, HR
back:
Relative Risk (RR): prospective studies; RR = (a/(a+b)) / (c/(c+d)); ratio of incidence rates
Odds Ratio (OR): case-control; OR = (a x d) / (b x c); approximates RR when event rare
Hazard Ratio (HR): survival analysis (Cox regression); ratio of hazard rates; time-to-event
Attributable Risk (AR) = RR - 1: how much of the risk is attributable to exposure
Population Attributable Risk (PAR): impact of exposure in entire population
All values: <1 = protective; 1 = null; >1 = increased risk

P-value: probability of observing results as extreme as (or more extreme than) the data, assuming H0 is true
Cutoff: p <0.05 = statistically significant (type I error <5%)
Limitations:
- Does NOT measure clinical significance
- Does NOT give magnitude of effect
- Can be significant for trivially small effects in large samples
- Statistical significance IS NOT clinical significance
Always interpret alongside: CI, effect size, NNT/NNH, clinical context

Systematic review: comprehensive literature search using predefined inclusion/exclusion criteria; PRISMA reporting guideline
Meta-analysis: pools data from multiple studies; provides summary effect estimate
Heterogeneity: I-squared statistic; I2 <25% = low; 25-75% = moderate; >75% = high (pooling problematic)
Forest plot: visual display of individual study and pooled effects; wider CI = less precise
Funnel plot: asymmetry suggests publication bias
Quality assessment: GRADE system (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation)

front: BNP and NT-proBNP in heart failure
back:
BNP (B-type natriuretic peptide): released from ventricles in response to pressure/volume overload
BNP <100 pg/mL: HF unlikely; BNP >500 pg/mL: HF likely
BNP 100-500 pg/mL: grey zone (consider other causes: sepsis, PE, renal failure)
NT-proBNP: age-adjusted thresholds:
- Age <50: >450 pg/mL
- Age 50-75: >900 pg/mL
- Age >75: >1800 pg/mL
Neprilysin (sacubitril) degrades BNP: BNP elevated on ARNI; use NT-proBNP to monitor HF status instead

A = Acidosis (refractory metabolic acidosis, pH <7.2)
E = Electrolyte (refractory hyperkalemia despite medical management)
I = Intoxication (certain dialyzable toxins: methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates, theophylline)
O = Overload (refractory fluid overload/pulmonary edema)
U = Uremia (symptoms: encephalopathy, pericarditis, coagulopathy, N/V unresponsive to treatment)
Dialyzability: small Vd, water-soluble, low protein-bound drugs (lithium, aminoglycosides, metformin)

Beta-lactamase production: destroys beta-lactam ring; carbapenemases (KPC, NDM, OXA)
Modified target sites: PBP2a in MRSA (mecA gene); altered penicillin-binding proteins in S. pneumoniae
Efflux pumps: actively pump antibiotics out of cell (FQs in Pseudomonas)
Permeability changes: porin loss (carbapenem resistance in Pseudomonas)
Ribosomal methylation: aminoglycoside resistance (rmt genes)
Enzymatic modification: acetyltransferases (aminoglycosides), phosphotransferases

Steady-state: achieved after 4-5 half-lives (independent of dose or dosing interval)
Steady-state concentration proportional to dose/CL (not to Vd)
Increasing dose frequency: narrows the peak-trough fluctuation (same average Css)
Decreasing dose frequency: widens fluctuation (same average Css)
Loading dose bypasses the wait for steady state
If CL changes (renal/hepatic dysfunction): Css changes proportionally; t1/2 and time to new steady state also change

1. Background: Tests explicit facts → memorization
2. Foreground: Applies facts to clinical scenario → tacit knowledge
3. Negative (NOT/EXCEPT/NEVER): Convert to T/F; false statement = correct answer
4. Conjunction (and/but/for/nor): Both parts must be correct; one wrong = eliminate
5. Two-Step: Solve Part 1 first; Part 2 depends on Part 1 answer
6. Bait & Switch: Identify ACTUAL question (usually last sentence); don't answer assumed question
7. Case-Based: Read ENTIRE case in order; look for pattern of association

80% Tacit knowledge = application of facts to real clinical scenarios
20% Explicit knowledge = memorized facts
90% Content = Patient care & translation of evidence into practice
10% Content = Specialty advancement
Timing: 60-70 seconds per question average
Format: Multiple-choice, single best answer only (no select-all-that-apply)
Mindset: Only well-established standards of practice are tested — not emerging or debated data

Step 1: Read each answer option
Step 2: Convert to a True/False statement
  → 'X IS [the thing asked about]'
Step 3: The FALSE statement is the correct answer

Example: 'Which is NOT a risk factor for diabetes?'
NSAID use → 'NSAID use IS a risk factor for diabetes' → FALSE → Correct Answer

Also: Eliminate extreme wording (always/never/must/only) — medicine is rarely absolute

1. Work in ORDER — never jump to the question first
2. Find the PATTERN of association between details
3. JUDGE information — evaluate labs/meds (don't just note them)
4. DON'T PRESUME — only accept stated facts
5. Consider the HOW — mechanism explains consequences
6. FORMULATE your answer BEFORE reading options (prevents distractor bias)

Tiebreaker: Mortality/QoL benefit wins between equal options
Lab Note: You WILL interpret CMP, CBC, TSH, lipids — know normal ranges

Trigger: See amiodarone in stem → expect one of these issues:
• Extremely long half-life (weeks–months)
• Drug interaction: ↑ Digoxin levels (reduce digoxin dose ~50%)
• Drug interaction: ↑ Warfarin (INR) effect — reduce warfarin dose
• Toxicities: Pulmonary fibrosis, Hepatotoxicity, QT prolongation
• Ocular: corneal microdeposits, optic neuropathy (vision changes)
• Thyroid: both hypo AND hyperthyroidism possible
• Skin: blue-gray photosensitivity
Question angle: usually rate/timing of administration OR monitoring for toxicities

Trigger: See clozapine → expect agranulocytosis question
• Mandatory ANC monitoring via REMS program
  - Baseline ANC ≥1500/mm³ before starting
  - Weekly monitoring for 6 months, then every 2 weeks, then monthly
  - ANC <500 = stop immediately
• Other toxicities: Metabolic syndrome (weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidemia), Myocarditis (highest risk first month), Seizures (dose-related)
• Clinical use: ONLY for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failed ≥2 antipsychotics)
• Most effective antipsychotic for reducing suicidality

front: Killer Foil: Mono (EBV) vs. Strep Pharyngitis
back:
SHARED presentation: Sore throat + fever + lymphadenopathy + tonsillar exudates

KEY DIFFERENTIATOR:
• Mono (EBV): POSTERIOR cervical lymphadenopathy + splenomegaly (LUQ pain)
• Strep (GABHS): ANTERIOR cervical lymphadenopathy only

Treatment:
• Mono → Supportive care ONLY (no antibiotics)
• Strep → Penicillin/Amoxicillin

CRITICAL TRAP: Amoxicillin given to mono patient → diffuse maculopapular rash. This is NOT an allergic reaction. No epinephrine/diphenhydramine needed — just stop amoxicillin. Absence of angioedema/dyspnea/GI sx = not allergy.

front: Killer Foil: Serotonin Syndrome vs. NMS
back:
SHARED: Hyperthermia + altered mental status + muscle rigidity

DIFFERENTIATOR:
• Serotonin Syndrome: Clonus + hyperreflexia + agitation + rapid onset (hours) + serotonergic drug causative
• NMS: Lead-pipe rigidity + bradykinesia + slow onset (days-weeks) + dopamine antagonist (antipsychotic) causative

Treatment:
• SS → Cyproheptadine + benzodiazepines + stop serotonergic agent
• NMS → Stop antipsychotic + bromocriptine/dantrolene + supportive care (cooling)

front: Killer Foil: HIT vs. Non-Immune Thrombocytopenia
back:
DIFFERENTIATOR:
• HIT: Platelet drop ≥50% occurring 5-10 days after heparin initiation + thrombosis (paradoxical clotting). Use 4Ts score.
  - 4Ts ≥4 = intermediate/high probability → stop heparin
• Non-immune (heparin-induced): Mild platelet drop within first 2 days — benign, does NOT require stopping heparin

Treatment of TRUE HIT:
• STOP all heparin (including line flushes, LMWH)
• Start non-heparin anticoagulant: argatroban (renal failure OK), bivalirudin, or fondaparinux
• Do NOT switch to warfarin alone (warfarin paradox: worsens thrombosis acutely)
• Avoid platelet transfusions

front: EBM on BPS Exams: Core Framework
back:
EBM = Best available evidence + Clinical expertise + Patient-specific factors (ALL EQUAL weight)

Study Hierarchy (strongest → weakest):
1. Systematic review / Meta-analysis
2. RCT (only design that proves causation)
3. Cohort study
4. Case-control study
5. Cross-sectional
6. Case report / Expert opinion

Key principle: Evidence alone is not sufficient. Patient values and clinical context matter equally. The BPS tests whether you can integrate all three pillars — not just recite guidelines.

front: Biostatistics Trap: Statistical vs. Clinical Significance
back:
Statistical significance (p<0.05): 5% chance result occurred by chance. Does NOT equal clinical importance.

Clinical significance: Is the effect size large enough to matter to patients?

CI Null-Crossing Rule:
• RR/OR crosses 1.0 → NOT statistically significant
• Mean difference crosses 0 → NOT statistically significant
• CI NOT crossing null → statistically significant

RRR vs. ARR Trap:
• RRR can be impressive even when ARR is tiny
• Example: 2% event rate → 1% with treatment = RRR 50%, ARR 1%, NNT 100
• Always report ARR — RRR without baseline risk misleads
• NNT = 1/ARR

• 70 seconds per question average — bank time on easy questions
• Never leave blank — 25% random chance; eliminate 1-2 → 50-75%
• If you must qualify your answer, it is likely wrong
• Longer answer options often correct (writers add qualifying language for clarity)
• Trust first instinct — don't change unless clear reason exists
• Maintain the mindset: only well-established guidelines tested, not controversial/emerging data
• Formulate your own answer BEFORE reading options (anti-distractor strategy)
• When panicking: go back to core concept — not a trick question

front: Vancomycin target AUC/MIC ratio for MRSA infections is [[400–600 mg·h/L]] (not trough alone per 2020 consensus).
back: 2020 ASHP/IDSA/SIDP Joint Consensus recommends AUC-guided vancomycin monitoring targeting AUC 400–600 mg·h/L for MRSA. Bayesian software is preferred for AUC estimation. Troughs of 15-20 mg/L alone are no longer recommended.

front: Piperacillin-tazobactam for Pseudomonas requires [[50% time >MIC]] PK/PD target and can be optimized by [[extended infusion over 3-4 hours]] for organisms with higher MICs.
back: Beta-lactam antibiotics are time-dependent killers. %T>MIC is the PK/PD driver. Extended infusion (3-4h) of pip-tazo increases the probability of target attainment against Pseudomonas with MICs of 8-16 mg/L.

front: For aminoglycosides, the PK/PD target is [[Cmax/MIC ≥8-10]] (concentration-dependent killing), and the dosing strategy that minimizes nephrotoxicity is [[extended interval / once-daily dosing]].
back: Aminoglycosides: concentration-dependent killers. Cmax/MIC ≥8-10 correlates with clinical cure. Once-daily extended interval dosing (Hartford nomogram) achieves high Cmax + allows low trough nadir → reduced nephrotoxicity vs. traditional q8h dosing.

front: The half-life of a drug with Vd = 70 L and CL = 7 L/hr is [[6.93 hours]] (t½ = 0.693 × Vd/CL).
back: t½ = 0.693 × Vd / CL = 0.693 × 70/7 = 6.93 hours. Full steady state is reached in ~4-5 half-lives (~28-35 hours). Time to steady state depends ONLY on half-life, not dose or dosing interval.

front: Sacubitril/valsartan (ARNI) requires a [[36-hour]] washout period from ACE inhibitor to prevent angioedema. No washout is needed when switching FROM [[ARB]] to ARNI.
back: Both ACEi and ARNIs inhibit bradykinin degradation. Co-administration causes angioedema. 36h washout allows bradykinin levels to normalize. ARBs don't affect bradykinin metabolism, so direct switch is safe.

front: Phenytoin has [[zero-order (saturable/Michaelis-Menten)]] kinetics at therapeutic concentrations, meaning small dose increases can cause [[disproportionately large]] increases in plasma level.
back: Phenytoin saturates hepatic metabolism (CYP2C9) at therapeutic concentrations → zero-order kinetics above ~10 mg/L. Michaelis-Menten kinetics: Vmax ~7 mg/kg/day, Km ~4 mg/L. A dose increase from 300→400mg/day can raise levels from 15 to 30 mg/L.

front: Heparin infusion: therapeutic aPTT target is [[60–100 seconds]] (or 1.5-2.5x normal). Protamine sulfate reversal: [[1 mg per 100 units]] of heparin given in last 2-4 hours.
back: Unfractionated heparin (UFH): aPTT 60-100s ≈ anti-Xa 0.3-0.7 units/mL. Protamine: 1 mg/100 units UFH administered. Protamine itself can cause bradycardia, hypotension, pulmonary hypertension — especially in patients with fish allergy or prior NPH insulin use.

front: 4T Score for HIT: Thrombocytopenia >50% drop (2 pts) + day [[5-10]] after heparin (2 pts) + thrombosis/skin necrosis (2 pts) + no other cause (2 pts). Score 6-8 = [[high]] probability.
back: HIT 4T score: Thrombocytopenia degree (0-2), Timing (0-2), Thrombosis (0-2), oTher cause unlikely (0-2). Score 0-3=low, 4-5=intermediate, 6-8=high. For high/intermediate: stop heparin, start non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban, fondaparinux, or bivalirudin).

front: For apixaban dose reduction in AF: use 2.5mg BID if ≥[[2]] of: age ≥80, weight ≤[[60 kg]], SCr ≥[[1.5 mg/dL]].
back: Apixaban (Eliquis) dose reduction criteria: 2 of 3 characteristics. Standard dose: 5mg BID. Reduced dose: 2.5mg BID. Reduces bleeding risk in small/elderly/renally impaired patients. Note: In VTE treatment, dose reduction criteria differ (not applicable to VTE dosing).

front: Warfarin + amiodarone interaction: amiodarone inhibits [[CYP2C9]] (primary warfarin metabolism) AND [[CYP3A4]], causing significantly increased INR. May need [[30-50%]] warfarin dose reduction.
back: Amiodarone is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9 (S-warfarin) and CYP3A4 (R-warfarin). Effect persists for weeks-months after amiodarone discontinuation due to its long t½ (40-55 days). INR must be monitored closely when starting, stopping, or dose-adjusting amiodarone.

front: Rifampin is a potent inducer of [[CYP3A4, CYP2C9, P-glycoprotein]]. It can reduce tacrolimus levels by up to [[90%]], requiring massive dose increases in transplant patients.
back: Rifampin induces hepatic and intestinal CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2B6, and P-gp. Effect starts within days, peaks 1-2 weeks. Tacrolimus Cmin can drop from therapeutic (8-12 ng/mL) to undetectable. Rifabutin is preferred for TB treatment in transplant patients (less potent inducer).

front: Serotonin syndrome clinical triad: [[mental status changes + autonomic instability + neuromuscular abnormalities (clonus, hyperreflexia)]]. Most commonly caused by [[combination of two serotonergic agents]].
back: Hunter Criteria for serotonin syndrome: clonus (inducible, spontaneous, or ocular) + at least one of: agitation, diaphoresis, tremor, hyperreflexia. Most common cause: SSRI/SNRI + tramadol, linezolid, methylene blue, or MAOi. Treatment: discontinue offending agents, cyproheptadine, benzos for agitation, supportive care.

front: Methotrexate + NSAIDs: NSAIDs reduce [[renal tubular secretion]] of methotrexate, increasing MTX levels and risk of [[myelosuppression and mucositis]]. Monitor CBC, renal function, and MTX levels.
back: MTX is primarily renally cleared via OAT3 transporters. NSAIDs compete for OAT3, reduce MTX clearance → elevated MTX. Risk is highest with high-dose MTX (oncology doses). Low-dose MTX (RA/psoriasis) carries lower but still real risk. Avoid NSAIDs with high-dose MTX.

front: Dabigatran MOA: direct [[thrombin (Factor IIa)]] inhibitor. Reversal agent: [[idarucizumab]] (Praxbind), a humanized monoclonal antibody Fab fragment.
back: Dabigatran (Pradaxa): direct thrombin inhibitor. Idarucizumab (Praxbind) binds dabigatran with ~350× higher affinity than thrombin. Approved for life-threatening/uncontrolled bleeding or urgent procedure. Dialyzable (~68% removed in 4h) unlike other DOACs.

front: Andexanet alfa reverses [[Factor Xa inhibitors]] (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban). It works as a [[recombinant modified Factor Xa decoy]] that sequesters Xa inhibitors.
back: Andexanet alfa (AndexXa): modified recombinant human Factor Xa without enzymatic activity. Acts as decoy to bind and sequester Xa inhibitors. FDA approved for life-threatening bleeding with rivaroxaban/apixaban. Very expensive. 4F-PCC is an alternative.

front: Spironolactone mechanism: [[aldosterone antagonist (potassium-sparing diuretic)]]. Main adverse effects: [[hyperkalemia]] and [[gynecomastia/breast tenderness]] (due to progestogenic/antiandrogenic activity).
back: Spironolactone competitively binds mineralocorticoid receptor. Cardiac benefits in HFrEF (RALES trial). Gynecomastia occurs in ~10-15% of male patients due to interaction with androgen receptors. Eplerenone is more selective (less gynecomastia). Monitor K+ and SCr within 1 week of initiation.

front: Mechanism of metformin: [[inhibits Complex I of hepatic mitochondrial respiratory chain]], reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis and increasing peripheral glucose uptake. Risk of [[lactic acidosis]] is contraindicated with eGFR [[<30]].
