back: Metformin: biguanide. Primary mechanism = inhibition of mitochondrial glycerophosphate dehydrogenase and Complex I → reduces hepatic gluconeogenesis. Weight neutral/weight loss. Hold for IV contrast if eGFR <60, restart when stable 48h later. Contraindicated eGFR <30.

front: GOLD COPD classification: FEV₁/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator confirms obstruction. GOLD 1=FEV₁≥[[80%]], GOLD 2=50-79%, GOLD 3=30-49%, GOLD 4=[[<30%]] predicted.
back: GOLD spirometric grades determine pharmacotherapy intensity. Groups A/B/C/D incorporate symptoms (CAT, mMRC) and exacerbation history. Group D (most severe) = ≥2 exacerbations or ≥1 leading to hospitalization per year = requires triple therapy (LABA+LAMA+ICS) ± roflumilast ± azithromycin.

front: AHA/ACC heart failure: HFrEF defined as EF ≤[[40%]]. HFmrEF = EF 41-49%. HFpEF = EF ≥[[50%]]. Only [[HFrEF]] has clear mortality-reducing pharmacotherapy (4 pillars).
back: HFrEF therapies with mortality benefit: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i. HFpEF: diuretics for congestion, SGLT2i (empagliflozin/dapagliflozin) show some benefit. HFmrEF: extrapolate from HFrEF data; growing evidence for GDMT.

front: CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥[[2]] in males or ≥[[3]] in females warrants oral anticoagulation in AF. DOACs preferred over warfarin except with [[mechanical heart valves or moderate-severe mitral stenosis]] (still require warfarin).
back: CHA₂DS₂-VASc: CHF(1), HTN(1), Age≥75(2), DM(1), Stroke/TIA(2), Vascular disease(1), Age 65-74(1), Sex female(1). DOACs (apixaban preferred) have better safety profile than warfarin for most AF patients. Exceptions: mechanical valves, MS (rivaroxaban trial showed harm in valvular AF).

front: Fluoroquinolones black box warning: [[tendinitis/tendon rupture]] (especially Achilles), [[peripheral neuropathy]], and [[aortic dissection/rupture]]. These risks are highest in elderly and [[corticosteroid users]].
back: FDA 2016 BBW update for fluoroquinolones includes: tendinitis/tendon rupture, peripheral neuropathy, CNS effects, QT prolongation, and aortic aneurysm/dissection. Use reserved for serious infections where benefit outweighs risk. Collagen degradation is the proposed mechanism for tendon/aortic effects.

front: Clozapine black box warnings: [[agranulocytosis]] (1-2% risk, requires REMS monitoring with ANC), [[seizures]] (dose-related), [[myocarditis]] (highest risk in first 4-8 weeks), and [[orthostatic hypotension]].
back: Clozapine REMS: ANC monitored weekly x6 months → every 2 weeks x6 months → monthly thereafter. Discontinue if ANC <1,000. Maximum dose associated with seizure risk ~600mg/day. Titrate slowly to minimize myocarditis risk. Used only for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and suicidality reduction.

front: Amiodarone black box warnings: [[pulmonary toxicity]] (most serious, 5-10%), [[hepatotoxicity]], and [[proarrhythmia (especially TdP)]]. Requires periodic [[chest X-ray, LFTs, PFTs, thyroid function tests]].
back: Amiodarone: Class III antiarrhythmic. PFTs + DLCO at baseline, then annually. LFTs every 6 months. TFTs every 3-6 months (both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism occur in 5-20%). Long t½ means ADRs persist long after discontinuation. Corneal microdeposits in nearly all patients (usually asymptomatic).

front: In a 2x2 contingency table, the Number Needed to Treat (NNT) = [[1 / Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR)]]. If drug reduces events from 10% to 7%, ARR = [[3%]], NNT = [[33]].
back: ARR = Control rate − Treatment rate = 10% − 7% = 3% = 0.03. NNT = 1/ARR = 1/0.03 = 33. Interpret as: need to treat 33 patients to prevent 1 event. Also: RRR = ARR/Control rate = 3%/10% = 30%. Both NNT and ARR depend on baseline risk (population-specific), while RRR does not.

front: Type I error (α) = [[falsely rejecting true null hypothesis (false positive)]]. Type II error (β) = [[failing to reject false null hypothesis (false negative)]]. Power = [[1 − β]].
back: α (significance level) = probability of Type I error = conventionally 0.05. β = probability of Type II error. Power = 1−β = probability of correctly detecting a true effect. Standard power ≥80% (β ≤20%). Increasing sample size reduces both α and β simultaneously.

front: Sensitivity = [[TP / (TP+FN)]] = ability to detect true disease (ruling OUT). Specificity = [[TN / (TN+FP)]] = ability to identify true non-disease (ruling IN). Mnemonic: [[SnNout / SpPin]].
back: Sensitivity: high → good for RULING OUT (if negative, disease unlikely). Specificity: high → good for RULING IN (if positive, disease likely). PPV and NPV depend on disease prevalence. LR+ = Sens/(1-Spec); LR− = (1-Sens)/Spec. LR >10 or <0.1 are diagnostically useful.

front: Acetaminophen overdose antidote: [[N-acetylcysteine (NAC)]]. It works by replenishing [[glutathione]], which detoxifies the toxic metabolite [[NAPQI]] (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine).
back: Acetaminophen toxic dose: >150 mg/kg acute. NAPQI (CYP2E1/3A4 product) depletes glutathione → hepatocellular necrosis. NAC: oral 140mg/kg load then 70mg/kg q4h×17 doses; or IV protocol. Rumack-Matthew nomogram guides treatment decision. Peak APAP level 4h post-ingestion.

front: Digoxin toxicity signs: [[bradycardia, AV block, visual changes (yellow-green halos)]], and GI symptoms. Antidote: [[digoxin-specific antibody Fab fragments (DigiFab/Digibind)]].
back: Digoxin: narrow therapeutic index (0.5-0.9 ng/mL for HFrEF; 0.8-2 ng/mL for AF rate control). Toxicity enhanced by hypokalemia (share K+ binding site on Na+/K+-ATPase). Digoxin Fab fragments: each vial binds ~0.5 mg digoxin. Dose based on estimated total body load or empirically (10 vials acute; 5 vials chronic toxicity).

front: Opioid overdose: naloxone dose is [[0.4 mg IV/IM/intranasal]] initially (can repeat q2-3 min). In fentanyl overdose, may need [[multiple higher doses]] (2-4mg) due to high potency. Duration of naloxone is [[30-90 min]] — shorter than most opioids.
back: Naloxone (Narcan): pure mu-opioid antagonist. Shorter duration than most opioids → re-dosing or infusion needed. Infusion: 2/3 of effective bolus dose per hour. Intranasal 4mg acceptable for bystander use. Caution in opioid-dependent patients: precipitates acute withdrawal.

front: Drug A has 90% first-pass metabolism. Bioavailability (F) = [[10%]]. If IV dose needed = 10mg, equivalent oral dose = [[100 mg]] (10mg / 0.10).
back: First-pass metabolism reduces oral bioavailability. F = 1 − extraction ratio. Oral dose equivalence: Dose_oral = Dose_IV / F. Example: morphine F ≈ 30% → oral dose must be ~3× IV dose for equianalgesia. Lidocaine: F ~3% (essentially no oral use for systemic effect).

front: Creatinine clearance (CrCl) by Cockcroft-Gault: [[((140-age) × weight × 0.85 if female) / (72 × SCr)]] in mL/min. Use [[actual body weight]] unless patient is obese (then use IBW or adjusted body weight).
back: Cockcroft-Gault: CrCl = [(140-age) × Wt] / (72 × SCr) × 0.85 if female. Used for drug dosing (FDA approved). IBW males = 50 + 2.3×(height inches - 60); females = 45.5 + 2.3×(height - 60). Adjusted BW for obese = IBW + 0.4×(actual-IBW). eGFR (MDRD/CKD-EPI) used for CKD staging, not drug dosing.

front: CURB-65 pneumonia severity: Confusion(1) + Urea >7 mmol/L(1) + RR ≥30(1) + BP <90/60(1) + Age ≥65(1). Score 0-1: [[outpatient]], 2: [[consider inpatient]], ≥3: [[ICU consideration]].
back: CURB-65 guides CAP treatment site. Score 0-1: low mortality (<2%), outpatient tx appropriate. Score 2: ~9% mortality, consider brief inpatient or supervised outpatient. Score 3-5: 17-57% mortality → inpatient/ICU needed. PSI/PORT score is more detailed alternative. Atypical coverage (macrolide or doxycycline) added for outpatient; respiratory fluoroquinolone or beta-lactam + azithromycin for inpatient.

front: SOFA score >[[2]] points from baseline indicates [[organ dysfunction]] in suspected infection (Sepsis-3 definition). qSOFA (bedside) = altered consciousness + RR ≥22 + SBP ≤[[100 mmHg]].
back: Sepsis-3 (2016): Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection (SOFA ≥2). Septic shock = sepsis + vasopressor requirement + lactate >2 mmol/L (mortality >40%). Old SIRS criteria removed from definition. Bundle: blood cultures, lactate, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h, 30mL/kg crystalloid.

front: Wells score DVT: Active cancer(1) + paralysis/immobilization(1) + recently bedridden >3d or surgery <4wk(1) + tenderness along deep vein(1) + entire leg swollen(1) + calf >3cm vs other leg(1) + pitting edema(1) + collateral superficial veins(1) + alternative diagnosis equally likely([[−2]]). Score >2=[[high probability]].
back: Wells DVT score: ≤0 = low (3% DVT prevalence), 1-2 = moderate (17%), ≥3 = high (75%). Low probability + negative D-dimer = DVT excluded (no imaging needed). Moderate/high: compression ultrasound needed. Low + positive D-dimer: ultrasound still needed.

front: Lithium therapeutic range: [[0.6–1.2 mEq/L]] (acute mania: 0.8–1.2; maintenance: 0.6–0.8). Toxicity risk at levels >[[1.5 mEq/L]]. NSAIDs, thiazides, and ACEi can [[increase lithium levels]].
back: Lithium: narrow therapeutic index. Check levels 12h after last dose. Early toxicity: tremor, polyuria, GI. Moderate (1.5-2): ataxia, confusion. Severe (>2): seizures, coma, permanent neurological damage. Sodium depletion increases lithium reabsorption. NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin synthesis → reduces GFR → increases lithium retention.

front: Methotrexate monitoring: CBC weekly for [[1 month]], then every [[1-2 months]] at steady state. Also monitor [[LFTs, BMP, and chest X-ray]]. Folate [[1 mg daily]] reduces GI toxicity and mucositis without reducing efficacy.
back: MTX (low-dose for RA/IBD): Hepatotoxicity (cumulative), pulmonary toxicity (MTX pneumonitis), myelosuppression. Folic acid 1mg/day or folinic acid (leucovorin) reduces toxicity. Leucovorin rescue for high-dose MTX toxicity. Avoid alcohol (additive hepatotoxicity). Withhold MTX if SCr rises significantly.

front: A drug given IV at 200mg produces Cmax = 10 mg/L. Volume of distribution = [[20 L]] (Vd = Dose/C0 = 200/10).
back: Apparent volume of distribution: Vd = Dose (mg) / C0 (mg/L). C0 is the back-extrapolated concentration at time zero for a one-compartment model. Large Vd (>60L or >1 L/kg) indicates extensive tissue distribution. Small Vd (<0.15 L/kg) suggests confinement to plasma.

front: In a one-compartment model, if t½ = 8 hours and the drug is given q12h, the accumulation ratio (R) = [[1 / (1 − e^(−0.693 × τ/t½))]] ≈ [[1.59]] (meaning steady-state Cmax is 1.59× single-dose Cmax).
back: Accumulation ratio R = 1 / (1 − e^(−ke × τ)). With t½=8h and τ=12h: ke=0.693/8=0.0866 hr⁻¹; e^(−0.0866×12) = e^(−1.04) = 0.354; R = 1/(1−0.354) = 1/(0.646) ≈ 1.55. This means significant accumulation occurs at q12h dosing with 8h half-life.

front: Fluoxetine has an active metabolite [[norfluoxetine]] with t½ of [[4-16 days]], making its drug interactions last [[4-6 weeks after stopping]] the drug.
back: Fluoxetine parent t½ ≈ 1-4 days; norfluoxetine t½ ≈ 4-16 days → effective washout takes 5-6 weeks. Critical for: (1) MAOi interactions (14-day washout required before MAOi after fluoxetine, but only 5 days for other SSRIs), (2) CYP2D6 inhibition persists after discontinuation. Contrast with sertraline t½ ≈ 26h.

front: Prasugrel is CONTRAINDICATED in: prior [[stroke/TIA]], age >[[75]] years (relative), and weight <[[60 kg]] (relative). It offers the most potent and predictable platelet inhibition of oral P2Y12 inhibitors.
back: Prasugrel (Effient): active metabolite formed via CYP enzymes (unlike clopidogrel, less susceptible to CYP2C19 polymorphism). TRITON-TIMI 38: superior to clopidogrel in ACS/PCI patients but with higher bleeding. No CYP2C19 variability concern. Absolute CI: prior stroke/TIA (net harm). Relative CI: age >75, weight <60kg.

front: Ticagrelor causes [[dyspnea]] in ~10-14% of patients (adenosine-mediated, not histamine) and should be avoided with [[aspirin doses >100mg/day]], which reduces ticagrelor's anti-platelet effect ('aspirin paradox').
back: Ticagrelor paradox: full-dose aspirin (325mg) reduces ticagrelor efficacy. PLATO trial found aspirin >100mg attenuated ticagrelor benefit. ACC/AHA recommend low-dose aspirin (81mg) with ticagrelor. Dyspnea: reversible, adenosine-related, occurs at rest/exertion — not bronchospasm. No dose reduction needed; usually self-limited.

front: Dofetilide requires in-hospital initiation for [[≥3 days]] due to QTc monitoring for [[Torsades de Pointes (TdP)]] risk. It is eliminated [[renally]] (80%), requiring dose reduction for CrCl <60 mL/min and is CONTRAINDICATED with CrCl <[[20 mL/min]].
back: Dofetilide (Tikosyn): Class III antiarrhythmic. Pure IKr (hERG) channel blocker → QT prolongation → TdP risk (dose-related). REMS program: prescribers must be trained. Dose: based on CrCl and baseline QTc. Contraindicated: CrCl <20, baseline QTc >440ms (>500ms in bundle branch block), concurrent drugs that prolong QT.

front: The EMPEROR-Reduced trial demonstrated that [[empagliflozin]] reduced the composite of CV death or HF hospitalization in HFrEF patients, making SGLT2 inhibitors the [[4th pillar]] of GDMT alongside ACEi/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA.
back: EMPEROR-Reduced: empagliflozin 10mg reduced primary endpoint by 25% vs placebo in HFrEF (EF ≤40%) regardless of diabetes status. DAPA-HF also showed benefit with dapagliflozin. Both dapagliflozin (Farxiga) and empagliflozin (Jardiance) are FDA approved for HFrEF. Key: eGFR ≥20 required (updated labeling).

front: RALES trial: spironolactone [[25mg daily]] reduced mortality by [[30%]] in severe HFrEF (EF <35%, NYHA III-IV) already on ACEi + loop diuretic. Main risks: [[hyperkalemia + gynecomastia]].
back: RALES (NEJM 1999): spironolactone vs placebo in severe HFrEF on standard therapy. 30% RRR in all-cause mortality, 35% RRR in HF hospitalization. Stopped early for benefit. Follow-up EPHESUS trial: eplerenone in post-MI LV dysfunction. EMPHASIS-HF: eplerenone in NYHA II HFrEF. Eplerenone preferred when gynecomastia occurs.
